macro_context.regime: Risk-on recovery. S&P 500 rebounding from April 2 Liberation Day tariff crash (-19% peak-to-trough). US-China 90-day tariff truce (May 12) holding: US tariffs on China at 30%, China tariffs on US at 10%. Market recovering aggressively — SPY opened the week at $585.99, closed week at $592.88 (+1.2%), hit 6,000 on S&P for first time since February by Friday.
macro_context.tariffs: Core market driver all week. US-China truce holding but fragile. 90-day window expires ~August 7. Market treating truce as positive but not fully pricing in permanent resolution. Consumer companies warning on tariff margin impact (LULU, DOCU indirectly). May ISM Manufacturing came in at 48.5% on June 2 — missed 49.3% estimate, stayed in contraction, tariff uncertainty cited by 100% of survey respondents. May ISM Services (June 4) came in at 49.9% — barely contraction, below 50 for only 4th time in 60 months.
macro_context.earnings_season: Q1 2025 earnings nearly done. 90%+ of S&P reported. 81-84% beat EPS, 26% YoY growth. Broadcom (AVGO) reporting June 5 after close — last major AI infrastructure earnings. LULU and DOCU also reporting June 5 after close.
macro_context.jobs_and_fed: May nonfarm payrolls (released Friday June 6): +139,000 vs 125,000 expected. Beat reduced recession fears, boosted equities. Unemployment stayed near historical lows. Fed held rates at 4.25-4.50% (May FOMC). Two cuts projected in dot plot by year-end. Rate cut in July essentially off the table by Friday given jobs strength. 10yr yield near 4.50%, 30yr breached 5.0% earlier in period.
macro_context.geopolitics: Trump-Putin 75-minute call June 4 (Ukraine focus). Trump-Xi 90-minute call June 5 (trade and tariffs — two agreed to continue negotiations). Both calls positive for market risk sentiment.
macro_context.key_catalyst_week: The Tesla-Musk-Trump public feud erupted June 5: Musk blasted Trump's 'Big Beautiful Bill' for eliminating EV tax credits. Trump fired back, threatened to review SpaceX/Tesla government contracts. TSLA dropped 14% in a single session — $152B market cap incinerated, biggest single-day market cap loss in Tesla history. By June 9 Musk-Trump rift showing early signs of easing, robotaxi news helped, TSLA bounced +8%.

trades.thesis: Tesla was trading at $342 — a rich valuation ($1.1T+ market cap) priced on Elon Musk's political capital as Trump insider and DOGE headliner. But this was a structurally precarious position: (1) Musk's core business at Tesla had been deteriorating — Q1 2025 deliveries fell YoY, margins compressed, European boycotts from Musk's political involvement already showing in sales data. (2) Musk had been publicly attacking Trump's 'Big Beautiful Bill' since late May, calling it a 'disgusting abomination.' This feud was building to a boiling point. The market was ignoring this political risk entirely. (3) Tesla's $7,500 EV credit was in the crosshairs of the bill — a direct $1.2B annual profit hit if credits were eliminated. The stock was priced for political immunity, not political jeopardy. Thesis: short TSLA into the Musk-Trump feud escalation, which was publicly visible and clearly heading toward a confrontation. The market was complacent on political risk for a company whose CEO was actively antagonizing the sitting president.
trades.catalyst: Trump-Musk feud escalation over EV tax credits in 'Big Beautiful Bill' — publicly telegraphed by late May, escalation imminent given Musk's increasingly aggressive X posts attacking the legislation
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: June 5 became the single most eventful day: TSLA crashed 14% in one session as Trump publicly threatened to review government contracts for Musk's companies. This was the exact political risk thesis playing out. Market had priced TSLA as a 'Trump trade' — when Trump publicly broke with Musk, the premium evaporated instantly. By June 9, early rift-healing signals (Musk sharing robotaxi test video, reports of White House-arranged call) started a bounce. The key insight: TSLA's valuation was not built on automotive fundamentals but on political optionality. Once that optionality was threatened, the stock repriced violently. The -14% day reduced market cap by $152B — the largest single-day market cap loss in Tesla's history.
trades.lesson: Political premium is the most dangerous premium to hold — it vaporizes instantly and without warning. TSLA was pricing Musk as a permanent White House insider, which was a thesis with a single point of failure: the relationship. The tell was that the fight was already visible in Musk's public X posts BEFORE June 5. Markets consistently underestimate political risk when the political relationship seems strong, then overcorrect when it breaks. The actionable version: when a CEO's political capital IS the valuation premium, any public deterioration of that relationship is the sell signal — not the bottom, but the start. Short thesis played through.

trades.thesis: Lululemon was trading at $323 heading into Q1 FY2025 earnings (reported June 5 after close). Thesis for a short: (1) Tariff exposure: LULU manufactures heavily in Asia. With US-China 30% tariff rate locked in, LULU's gross margins faced direct headwinds. Previous guidance from February already showed pressure. (2) Americas same-store sales had been negative or flat for multiple quarters — the US consumer was tapped out at the premium athleisure price point. (3) The stock had bounced ~15% from April lows on tariff truce optimism, but this bounce didn't reflect that LULU's margins were structurally impaired regardless of tariff outcomes — 30% tariff rate is NOT relief, it's damage. (4) Valuation: LULU at $323 was ~24x forward earnings, still premium for a company with decelerating US comps and rising COGS. (5) Pattern: consumer discretionary with direct Asia manufacturing exposure was the most vulnerable sector to tariff cuts to earnings — LULU fit this profile precisely. Risk: China comps growing 8%+ could offset if China becomes the growth story. But investor focus was Americas.
trades.catalyst: Q1 FY2025 earnings June 5 after close — expected to show full tariff margin pressure in guidance even if Q1 beat on the top line
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: LULU reported June 5: Q1 revenue +7% to $2.4B (beat), EPS $2.60 (beat). But management cut full-year EPS guidance to $14.58-14.78 from $14.95-15.15 AND projected gross margin down 110bps vs prior guidance of 60bps — the entire incremental 50bps was explicitly attributed to tariffs. CFO said selective price increases would come in Q3. Market interpreted this as: tariff headwind is real, Q2 will be the worst quarter for margins before any price increases kick in, and they've lost pricing power in Americas. Stock opened June 6 at $271 from $330 close — a 18% overnight gap. Continued selling through the week, hitting $259 by June 9. By the 20-day exit at $238, LULU had fallen -26% from entry. The brutal reality: LULU's guidance cut was essentially management saying 'we cannot fully pass through tariff costs to our American customers' — a massive thesis confirmer for the short.
trades.lesson: Tariff impact is a tangible, quantifiable headwind for consumer companies with Asian manufacturing. The market in early June was still in 'the truce is good enough' mode — but 30% tariffs on China goods still destroy margins for companies that can't easily move supply chains or raise prices. The lesson is timing: when a company with direct tariff exposure is about to report, their guidance will reflect the full tariff reality even if their Q1 revenue beat is clean. The gap between 'Q1 beat' and 'guidance cut' is where the trade lives. LULU's Americas comp decline was the critical second data point — you can't price-increase your way out of -1% comps. The combination of tariff headwind + weak pricing power = inevitable margin compression = inevitable multiple derating.

trades.thesis: DocuSign was trading at $89 heading into Q1 FY2026 earnings (June 5 after close). Thesis: (1) Billings deceleration: DocuSign's billings — the forward-looking demand metric — had been consistently disappointing. The company was in a difficult transition: trying to expand beyond e-signatures into 'Intelligent Agreement Management' (IAM) while its core e-signature market was approaching saturation. (2) Go-to-market restructuring: DOCU had recently reorganized its sales force, which typically creates transition-period weakness in renewal timing and net new ARR. (3) The stock had run +30% from April lows on the general market recovery, pricing in a clean quarter. But billings growth was expected to be sub-7% — barely covering the price-to-sales multiple of ~4x that the market was paying. (4) Competition from Adobe Acrobat Sign and emerging AI-native document workflow tools was putting structural pressure on DOCU's moat. (5) Valuation: at $89, the stock was pricing perfection for a company in the middle of a business model transition — dangerous combination.
trades.catalyst: Q1 FY2026 earnings June 5 after close — focus on billings guidance which was the key bull-bear debate
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: DOCU reported June 5: EPS $0.90 vs $0.81 est (beat). Revenue $764M vs $748M est (beat). But billings came in at $739.6M vs $746M expected — a miss. Full-year billings guidance was CUT to $3.28-3.34B from prior $3.30-3.35B. Q2 revenue growth guided at 6% — decelerating. Management attributed the billings weakness to 'timing of renewals' from go-to-market restructuring. Market saw through the 'it's timing not demand' narrative and sent the stock down 18% on June 6 (from $93.84 to $75.28). The 20-day exit at $77.95 locked in -12.6% from entry. The market was trading: billings is the one metric DOCU needs to nail to justify the valuation, and they missed AND cut guidance. The 'timing not demand' excuse, delivered in the middle of an IAM pivot, was not credible enough to stop the selling.
trades.lesson: For SaaS companies in transition, billings is king — not revenue, not EPS. When a company beats on revenue and EPS but misses billings and cuts billings guidance, the market treats it as a demand problem regardless of management's 'timing' narrative. DOCU's mistake was using the earnings call to explain why billings was soft ('go-to-market restructuring timing') in the same quarter they're pivoting to IAM — the market couldn't disentangle 'operational transition timing' from 'demand destruction.' The short worked because the setup was: high valuation + business model transition + billings deceleration = three strikes.

trades.thesis: AMD was trading at $114.63 — significantly underperforming NVDA in the AI chip narrative. The thesis for a long here: (1) Broadcom's Q2 earnings (June 5) were going to confirm sustained AI infrastructure spending — hyperscaler capex was still expanding. This was a rising tide for the entire AI semiconductor space. (2) AMD's MI300X was gaining traction with hyperscalers as a cost-competitive alternative to NVDA H100/H200. Azure, Google, and Meta all had deployed MI300X at meaningful scale by Q1 2025. (3) AMD's gaming segment bottomed and was turning — not a major driver but removes a headwind. (4) Valuation: AMD at 23x forward P/E was a significant discount to NVDA at 35-40x on a PEG basis, with AMD growing earnings faster relative to its multiple. (5) The US-China tariff truce positive for AMD's data center sales cycle — hyperscalers had paused some decisions during April-May uncertainty, and the truce reopened capex. (6) Catalyst timing: AMD's own analyst day expected later in June/July, plus any Broadcom AI upside would lift AMD as second-order beneficiary.
trades.catalyst: Broadcom earnings June 5 as AI infrastructure confirmation, US-China trade truce holding, AMD MI300X momentum data in upcoming conferences
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: AMD was trading the AI chip broadening narrative. While NVDA led the AI infrastructure story, AMD was being accumulated by investors who saw it as the second-best house in a great neighborhood. The week of June 2-9 saw AMD outperform: +6.2% on the week vs SPY +1.2%. The Broadcom earnings June 5 confirmed hyperscaler AI capex was not slowing — AVGO AI revenue +46% YoY, guided $5.1B Q3. This was direct signal that AMD's MI300X customer base was still spending. By July 8, AMD hit $137.82 — a +20% return in 20 trading days. The market also started pricing in AMD's Instinct MI350 roadmap and competitive positioning versus NVDA's Blackwell. The trade benefited from three concurrent tailwinds: AI sector momentum, tariff truce risk-on, and AMD-specific MI300X order pipeline.
trades.lesson: When the sector leader (NVDA) delivers blowout results but trades sideways on sell-the-news, the second-tier names often outperform. AMD at a meaningful P/E discount to NVDA, with real MI300X deployments at hyperscalers, was the better risk/reward. The lesson: in a strong thematic trade, find the laggard in the same theme with a catalyst that the leader's results directly confirm. AVGO's AI revenue acceleration was a direct bullish read-through for AMD, but AMD had to absorb that positive independently — hence the outperformance opportunity.

trades.thesis: Broadcom reported after close June 5: Q2 AI revenue +46% YoY to $4.4B, total revenue $15.0B (+20% YoY), Q3 revenue guided at $15.8B. These were genuinely strong numbers. But the stock dropped 6% on June 6 and June 9 for a technical reason: gross margin guidance for Q3 was going to decline ~130bps sequentially because of a higher mix of custom AI accelerators (XPUs — chips designed for Meta, Google, Apple) versus software. The market interpreted this as 'AI margins are compressing' — but that was a misread. Custom XPUs at scale ARE AVGO's moat. The margin dilution was a mix-shift story, not a competitive pricing story. Entry thesis on June 5 close/June 6 dip: (1) This is a sell-the-news reaction to a genuinely beat quarter with strong forward guidance. (2) The margin concern is a mix-shift issue, not structural margin erosion — XPU customers are sticky (multi-year contracts), just lower gross margin than AVGO's networking software. (3) At $250 post-earnings (down from $260), AVGO was trading at a discount to its AI growth trajectory. (4) The AVGO AI revenue doubling roadmap ($4.4B → $5.1B in one quarter) was not fully priced.
trades.catalyst: Post-earnings dip following beat quarter; gross margin concern was timing/mix-shift, not structural; AI revenue growth trajectory intact
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: The AVGO post-earnings reaction was a textbook 'sell-the-news-then-recover' pattern. Stock ran from $241 on June 2 to $259 on June 4 (pre-earnings run-up), then gapped down to $250 on June 5 (earnings day itself choppy as market digested the results), then sold off to $245 on June 6 and $242 on June 9. The 'problem' the market focused on — gross margin dilution from XPU mix — was real but temporary and was a feature, not a bug: it meant AVGO was winning more XPU business (Meta, Google custom AI chips). By June 11-12, AVGO was already recovering to $250-254. By July 8 it hit $270. The market eventually realized: 46% AI revenue growth with a clear path to doubling is more important than 130bps of gross margin noise.
trades.lesson: When a company beats on every key metric but the stock sells off on one technical concern (here: gross margin mix-shift), the opportunity is to buy the dip if you can correctly diagnose whether the concern is structural or temporary. AVGO's XPU margin dilution was temporary and was actually a sign of product strength — they were winning more custom chip business. The post-earnings dip created a 10% opportunity in 20 trading days. The lesson: distinguish between 'bad earnings with bad guidance' (don't buy the dip) vs 'great earnings with one legitimate but temporary concern' (buy the dip). AVGO was clearly the latter.

pre_open_news: Memorial Day weekend just passed — first full week of June
US-China 90-day tariff truce (May 12) holding: US at 30% on Chinese goods, China at 10% on US goods
ISM Manufacturing for May releasing at 10am ET — expected 49.3%, prior 48.7%
S&P 500 near 5,820 (SPY ~$585.99) — recovering from April crash, now up ~15% from April 9 low
Earnings calendar: AVGO, LULU, DOCU all reporting Thursday June 5 after close
key_events: ISM Manufacturing May: 48.5% vs 49.3% expected — MISS. Stayed in contraction. New orders contracted for 4th straight month. 100% of survey respondents cited tariffs. New Export Orders and Imports hit Great Recession-level lows.
Market opened flat, slight weakness on ISM miss, but quickly recovered as investors viewed the data as backward-looking and the tariff truce as forward-looking positive
SPY closed at $585.99 — essentially flat on the day
Market rallied on tariff optimism and rotation into tech
SPY +0.7%, QQQ +0.95% on the day
LULU pre-earnings run: stock hit $334.91 (+3.7%) on speculative covering ahead of earnings — short thesis temporarily painful
AVGO pre-earnings run: $254.79 (+3.3%) — market pricing in a big beat
AMD +2.3% — tech rally broadly
No major economic data
daily_log.commentary: The LULU run-up to $334 was the short's maximum pain point pre-earnings. The thesis was intact — in fact, the higher it ran into earnings, the bigger the post-earnings drop would be if guidance disappointed. Held short.

key_events: ISM Services May: 49.9% — barely below 50 (contraction). New Orders index fell to 46.4% — contracted for first time since June 2024. 4th time in 60 months services hit contraction territory.
Market digested services PMI weakness but maintained bid — investors more focused on Thursday earnings
Trump-Putin 75-minute call on Ukraine: constructive tone, no major announcements but removed tail risk
AVGO continued pre-earnings run to $258.98 (+1.6%)
TSLA continued decline: $332.05 (-3.6%) — Musk's X posts attacking the Big Beautiful Bill were escalating publicly
SPY: $589.11 roughly
daily_log.commentary: Services PMI dipping below 50 was notable — the services sector had been the economy's shock absorber while manufacturing contracted. Two sectors now simultaneously soft = recessionary signal if sustained. But market shrugged because: (1) one month of services contraction not definitive, (2) jobs data coming Friday would be more definitive. The TSLA decline on June 4 was an early signal the Musk-Trump fight was building. The public nature of Musk's attacks on Trump's bill meant escalation was likely.

key_events: PRE-MARKET: Musk-Trump feud fully erupts. Trump says Musk 'wearing thin,' threatens to review SpaceX/Tesla government contracts. Musk fires back on X with 'Whatever.'

TSLA CRASHES 14% intraday on the Musk-Trump news — from $335 to $284. Market cap falls $152B — largest single-day market cap loss in Tesla history. EV credit elimination in 'Big Beautiful Bill' = $1.2B annual profit hit.
Trump-Xi 90-minute call on trade and tariffs — both sides agreed to continue negotiations. Risk-on catalyst. Rest of market rallied while TSLA crashed.

AFTER CLOSE — BROADCOM (AVGO) Q2 Results: Revenue $15.0B (+20% YoY) ✓ | AI revenue $4.4B (+46% YoY) ✓ | Q3 guidance $15.8B (+21%) ✓ | BEAT all estimates | BUT Q3 gross margin guided down 130bps sequentially on XPU mix. Stock initially up AH then gave back gains on margin concern.

AFTER CLOSE — LULULEMON (LULU) Q1 Results: Revenue $2.4B (+7%) ✓ | EPS $2.60 ✓ | BEAT top/bottom line | BUT full-year EPS cut to $14.58-14.78 from $14.95-15.15 | Gross margin now expected down 110bps vs prior 60bps — delta is tariffs | Americas comp -1% | Stock CRASHES 17-20% in AH

AFTER CLOSE — DOCUSIGN (DOCU) Q1 FY26 Results: EPS $0.90 vs $0.81 est ✓ | Revenue $764M vs $748M est ✓ | BEAT top/bottom | BUT billings $739.6M vs $746M expected — MISS | Full-year billings cut to $3.28-3.34B from $3.30-3.35B | Q2 growth guided at 6% (decelerating) | Stock CRASHES 18% AH
SPY closed roughly flat on the day: $589.32 (Musk-Trump/TSLA weighed vs Trump-Xi call positive)
daily_log.commentary: June 5 was the most information-dense day of the period. Three major earnings all reported same day. TSLA and LULU gave us the biggest moves. The market on this day was clearly trading: (1) political premium evaporation for TSLA, (2) tariff margin reality for consumer names, (3) SaaS billings deceleration for DOCU, and (4) AVGO's AI story is real but priced with a temporary margin concern. The Trump-Xi call was positive for the broader market and helped offset the Tesla drag on sentiment.

key_events: BEFORE OPEN — May Nonfarm Payrolls: +139,000 vs 125,000 expected — BEAT. Unemployment rate fell to 4.1% from 4.2%. Average hourly earnings +0.2% MoM, +3.7% YoY. Strong report — removed near-term recession fears. Stocks surged on open.
LULU opened at $271 (down from $330 close) — gap down 18%. Continued selling through the day, closed $265.27. Historic worst-day-ever territory. Trading 16M shares vs normal 1-2M.
DOCU opened at $76.78 (down from $93.84 close) — gap down 18.2%. Closed $75.28. Trading 22M shares vs normal 2-3M.
AVGO opened at $249.12 (down from $257.84) — gap down 3.4%. Sell-the-news on margin concern. Closed $244.95.
TSLA bounced slightly: $295.14 (+3.7% from $284.70 close). Early signs Musk-Trump rift showing first easing signals. Robotaxi test video shared on X.
S&P 500 closed above 6,000 for first time since February — key psychological milestone.
SPY: $592.88 close. Week gain: +1.2%. Nasdaq: +2.2%.
Jobs strength pushed July rate cut odds to ~5% from 24% — hawkish recalibration but market took it well (strong jobs = no recession).
Dow Jones erased ALL 2025 year-to-date losses by Friday close.
daily_log.commentary: The jobs beat was the week's cleanest macro catalyst. Strong employment with contained wage growth is the Goldilocks scenario. Markets correctly read it as: recession risk declining, earnings resilient, Fed can stay patient. The LULU/DOCU collapses were the most extreme earnings reactions of the week — both down 18%+ on guidance cuts. Importantly, both gaps happened in after-hours on June 5 — by June 6 market open, the damage was already done. The lesson: for earnings shorts, you capture the AH move if you have puts expiring right after earnings, but a stock short captures the continued selling through the session and following days.

key_events: Gap higher on continued momentum from strong jobs report

Tesla bounced hard: +8% open from $295 close to $308.58. Reports emerged of White House-arranged call between Musk and Trump showing willingness to reconcile. Robotaxi Austin driverless testing news also positive.
AMD surged: $119.18 open, closed $121.73 (+4.7% from Friday) — AI chip rotation continued
AVGO continued weak: $242.32 — still absorbing the post-earnings margin concern
LULU continued lower: $259.04 (-2.4% from Friday's already-crashed level)
DOCU slight recovery: $78.58 from $75.28 — short covering after 18% single-day drop
Market overall: SPY +0.5%, QQQ +0.65%
daily_log.commentary: June 9 showed the divergence patterns clearly: TSLA bouncing on rift-healing news, AMD continuing to run on AI momentum, while LULU/DOCU remained suppressed. The week ended with S&P above 6,000, Dow YTD positive, and the market firmly in recovery mode driven by tariff truce + strong jobs + AI infrastructure confirmation from AVGO.

period_summary.context: Week of June 2-9, 2025 was a pivotal transition week for the 2025 market recovery. The S&P 500 crossed 6,000 for the first time since February. The week was defined by: (1) tariff truce holding but showing asymmetric impact across companies, (2) the Musk-Trump feud as the week's biggest political shock, (3) triple earnings event on June 5 after close creating massive individual stock dislocations, and (4) a strong May jobs report removing near-term recession fears.

trade_outcomes.T01_TSLA_short: WIN +13.1% — Thesis: political premium evaporation. Played out exactly as anticipated when Trump-Musk feud exploded June 5. -14% single session, held short for 20 days to $297.81.
trade_outcomes.T02_LULU_short: WIN +26.1% — Thesis: tariff margin destruction + weak Americas pricing power. Confirmed by Q1 earnings guidance cut. Stock went from $322 to $238 in 20 trading days.
trade_outcomes.T03_DOCU_short: WIN +12.6% — Thesis: billings deceleration + business model transition risk. Confirmed by billings miss and full-year billings cut. Stock went from $89 to $77.
trade_outcomes.T04_AMD_long: WIN +20.2% — Thesis: AI chip laggard with NVDA/AVGO results as confirming catalyst. AMD went from $114 to $137 in 20 trading days.
trade_outcomes.T05_AVGO_long_post_dip: WIN +10.3% — Thesis: post-earnings dip on temporary gross margin concern, not structural. AVGO went from $244 to $270 in 20 trading days.

macro_lessons: Tariff impact is ASYMMETRIC by company — 30% tariff truce was a positive for market broadly but a disaster for consumer companies with Asian manufacturing (LULU) that couldn't fully pass through costs. The truce did not eliminate the tariff — it just reduced it from 145% to 30%. For LULU's margins, 30% was still devastating.

Political premium is the most dangerous premium — when the political relationship underpinning the premium breaks, the repricing is instant and violent. TSLA's valuation was partially built on Musk-as-Trump-insider. One public fight removed $152B in one session.

For earnings shorts in tariff-exposed companies: the Q1 EPS beat is a red herring. The real tell is the GUIDANCE. Companies with direct manufacturing exposure to China will cut full-year guidance even when Q1 beats because they can quantify the tariff damage for the rest of the year. LULU knew in June that their Q2/Q3 gross margins were going to be compressed by tariffs.

SaaS company billings is the truth serum — beats on revenue and EPS mean nothing if billings guidance is cut. DOCU's beat-then-cut pattern was a classic sign of deteriorating forward demand being masked by current-period performance.

Strong jobs data in a recovery environment is unambiguously bullish for equities — the market correctly read May +139K jobs as: recession avoided, consumer income holding, Fed patient, earnings estimates stable.

The AI infrastructure spend confirmation from AVGO (+46% AI revenue YoY, guiding $5.1B Q3) was a direct positive read-through for the broader AI chip complex — AMD, NVDA, and even SMCI all benefited from this secular confirmation.

Post-earnings dips driven by single-metric concerns (gross margin mix-shift for AVGO) are often buying opportunities when the overall trajectory is intact. The market's initial sell-the-news reaction to AVGO was wrong on fundamentals — it corrected to $270 within 20 days.
period_summary.market_structure_observation: The week demonstrated a classic bifurcation: the market as a whole was in recovery mode (AI + tariff truce + jobs = bullish), but within that broad rally, there was brutal stock-specific destruction in tariff-exposed consumer and decelerating SaaS names. The key skill was identifying WHICH names would face reality check vs which would benefit from the recovery. The answer: benefit = AI infrastructure (AMD, AVGO recovery), AI-adjacent (META, GOOGL up); reality check = tariff-exposed consumer discretionary (LULU), transitioning SaaS with billings deceleration (DOCU), politically exposed names (TSLA).
